Damage results in ptosis, "down and out" eye deviation, mydriasis, and loss of accommodation, but does not impair afferent input from the retina. An oculomotor
                                                                                                            palsy would result in visible ophthalmoplegia and anisocoria rather than visual loss and RAPD. This patient lacks findings of extraocular muscle weakness or ptosis, excluding oculomotor nerve damage.
                                                                                                            Choice C: The trochlear nerve innervates the superior oblique muscle, contributing to depression and intorsion of the adducted eye. Trochlear nerve lesions cause vertical diplopia, particularly when looking down and in, but do not affect vision, pupillary responses, or light reflex afference. Isolated trochlear nerve palsy would present with head tilt and

Choice B: Germinomas are midline tumors that can arise in the pineal or suprasellar region, but they are more common in adolescent males and often cause precocious puberty (if hCG-secreting) or Parinaud syndrome (if pineal). They are not typically calcified with cystic components or associated with bitemporal hemianopsia.
                                                                                                        Choice C: Optic gliomas affect the optic nerves and chiasm and can present with visual disturbances, especially in patients with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1). column2:

Choice B: Huntington disease is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by a trinucleotide (CAG) repeat expansion in the HTT gene on chromosome 4. It presents with a triad of progressive choreiform movements, psychiatric disturbances (such as depression or irritability), and cognitive decline. Neuropathologically, there is
                                                                                                           degeneration of the caudate nucleus and putamen, with accompanying cortical atrophy. The disease typically manifests in mid-adulthood and worsens over 15–20 years. Huntington disease results from toxic gain-of-function of the huntingtin protein, not from iron deficiency or dopamine biosynthesis dysfunction. While both conditions may involve the
                                                                                                           basal ganglia, the movement features of Huntington disease are involuntary and hyperkinetic, unlike the voluntary leg movement behaviors seen in RLS. There is no increased risk of Huntington disease in patients with restless legs syndrome.
